sections.content:
**Constraints produce better outputs than freedom.** This is counterintuitive but consistent.

When you give AI unlimited latitude, it defaults to safe, generic, comprehensive answers — the textbook response. When you impose constraints, AI must make choices that produce more focused, creative, and useful results.

Powerful constraint types:

- **Length constraints:** "In exactly 3 sentences" / "Under 50 words" / "2 paragraphs max"
- **Format constraints:** "No bullet points" / "Write as a dialogue" / "Use only plain English"
- **Exclusion constraints:** "Don't mention price" / "No technical jargon" / "Avoid using the word 'leverage'"
- **Audience constraints:** "For someone who has never heard of us" / "For a CFO who only cares about ROI"
- **Perspective constraints:** "Argue the opposite of my position" / "As a skeptic" / "As our most critical customer"

Constraints also prevent AI's tendency toward exhaustive lists. Asking for "the 3 most important" instead of "all the important" forces prioritization — which is usually what you actually need.

sections.bad: Give me feedback on my strategy document.
sections.good: Give me feedback on this strategy document. Constraints: (1) only identify the 3 most critical gaps — not every possible improvement, (2) frame each gap as a question the document doesn't answer, (3) no more than 2 sentences per gap, (4) if you think the strategy is fundamentally flawed, say that first.
sections.explanation: Without constraints, AI gives exhaustive generic feedback that's hard to prioritize. With constraints, you get exactly 3 critical gaps, framed as questions, in a digestible format — plus a 'fundamentally flawed' flag if needed. The constraints force the output to be immediately actionable.

sections.prompt: When you ask AI for 'all the important considerations' instead of 'the ___ most important,' AI defaults to ___ lists that require additional ___ from you.

data.hint: Think about what happens when there's no upper limit on how many items to include.

sections.prompt: Which constraint will most improve this prompt: "Write a value proposition for our software"?

choices: "Write a good value proposition for our software"
"Write a value proposition for our software. One sentence only. No technical terms. Must include a specific measurable outcome (e.g., saves X hours, reduces Y by Z%)."
"Write a detailed value proposition for our software"
"Write a comprehensive value proposition for our software covering all features"
data.explanation: The second option adds three powerful constraints: length (one sentence), language (no jargon), and quality bar (specific measurable outcome). These constraints force AI to make the hard choices — prioritizing, simplifying, quantifying — that produce genuinely useful marketing copy rather than generic filler.
